briefing.one_line_description: Financial infrastructure platform enabling businesses to accept payments and offer financial services globally.

recent_signals.description: Tender offer at $159B valuation led by Thrive Capital, Coatue, a16z for employee liquidity
DoorDash partnership to power global stablecoin payments via Stripe-backed Tempo blockchain in 40+ countries
Launched Agentic Commerce Suite and shopping integration with ChatGPT and Microsoft Copilot
Revenue suite (Billing, Invoicing, Tax) on track to reach $1B annual run rate in 2026
Laid off 300 employees but plans 17% headcount growth to reach 10,000 employees by year-end

data_gaps: Total funding amount (private company, not disclosed)
Last funding date (tender offer is employee liquidity, not primary funding)
Complete C-suite roster (CFO, CTO, CMO not mentioned)
More granular tech stack details beyond partnerships

dossier.disqualifier_reason: Core business model is not software — Stripe is a fintech/payments infrastructure company that derives revenue primarily from transaction fees and payment volume (percentage cuts on processed payments) rather than subscription licenses for software. While they offer APIs and developer tools, their fundamental revenue model is based on payment processing volume, not SaaS subscriptions.
dossier.tier_reasoning: Stripe trips the 'Core business model is not software' disqualifier. Despite having software APIs and developer tools, their primary revenue comes from transaction fees and payment processing volume (percentage cuts), not subscription software licenses. This places them in the fintech/payments infrastructure category explicitly listed as a disqualifier, alongside Adyen, Plaid, and Square.
icp_gaps: Not a B2B SaaS company — revenue model is transaction-based, not subscription software
Fintech/payments infrastructure falls under explicit disqualifier category
Business model fundamentally incompatible with ICP definition
